title: Vintage Camper Napkin Holder
summary: A whimsical vintage-camper-shaped napkin holder cut from scrap wood, with a dowel tongue jack and drilled wheel details.
description:
This is a novelty-shape project first and a napkin holder second — the whole point is the silhouette, cut from a printed pattern rather than measured out board by board. Scrap wood is genuinely appropriate here since the piece is small and gets fully painted, so grain and species don't matter the way they would on a stained piece.

The dowel **"tongue jack"** isn't just decorative — it's what holds the front of the camper level on a table, exactly like a real travel trailer's jack holds its front end level when unhitched.

Drilled wheel details, cut with a hole saw on a drill press, are what sell the vintage-camper look at a glance — plain edges would read as an abstract shape rather than a specific object.

tools.note: Or a scroll saw for the curved camper body.
Cuts clean circular wheel details with a hole saw.

steps.title: Transfer the pattern
steps.body: Print the camper-shaped pattern and transfer it onto scrap wood.

steps.title: Cut the body and base
steps.body: Cut the curved camper body shape with a jigsaw, then cut the napkin holder's bottom to length on a miter saw.

steps.title: Add the tongue jack
steps.body: Drill a pilot hole and glue in the dowel tongue jack so it holds the front of the camper level.

steps.title: Drill the wheel details
steps.body: Use a drill press and hole saw to cut circular tire details into the body.

steps.title: Assemble and finish
steps.body: Assemble the sides and tire details with a brad nailer and glue, then paint in a retro color scheme.
